text: This is a video of single site multiport surgery of laparoscopic right hemicolectomy for carcinoma cecum. This video is coming from Gem hospital Coimbatore.
The surgeon places one 10 mm and two 5 mm laparoscopic trocars through a 3.5 cm incision along the umbilicus, followed by an initial inspection of the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon positions the transverse colon upwards and lifts the ileum to create a retroperitoneal tunnel behind the cecum and ascending colon, manipulating the cecal mesentery.

The surgeon begins dissection by dividing the root of the mesentery attachment, entering the avascular plane between the mesentery and the retroperitoneum. The right gonadal vessel is visible, and the lateral attachment of the cecum and ascending colon remains intact. The right ureter is identified, and the peritoneal incision extends medially to the root of the mesentery.

The surgeon dissects the avascular plane between the mesentery and retroperitoneum, with clear visibility of the right gonadal vessels. The left hand instrument pushes the colon cranially to stretch the plane, facilitating further dissection while preserving the layer over the right ureter and gonadal vessel.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter, gonadal vessel, and duodenum, ensuring these structures are preserved without injury. The right ureter is clearly visible, and the protective layer over it is maintained.

The surgeon creates a tunnel above the duodenum, revealing the head of the pancreas. The mesenteric attachment of the right side colon is lifted, providing clear visibility of the anatomical structures.

A gauze piece is placed for identification during anterior dissection, revealing the duodenum clearly. The surgeon dissects tissue around the duodenum, with the mesentery repositioned. Prominent vessels are visible following posterior dissection, and an incision is made along the superior mesenteric pedicle.

The surgeon skeletonizes the ileocolic vessel, carefully dissecting multiple lymph nodes from around the vessel towards the specimen side. The nodes are removed from the origin of the ileocolic pedicle.

The surgeon lifts the vessel and performs dissection behind it, completing the retroperitoneal dissection. Both the artery and vein are individually clipped using a Fiamam disposable clip applicator. The vein is divided with the Fiamam ligasure, followed by the division of the artery after double clipping.

The surgeon lifts the pedicle and enters the dissected area, revealing the inferior vena cava (IVC) clearly. A gauze piece aids in identifying the retroperitoneal dissected area, marking the extent of the dissection.

The surgeon identifies the middle colic artery, noting the absence of a right colic vessel. The right and left branches of the middle colic artery are visible. The right branch is skeletonized, clipped, and divided using a ligature coagulation device.
The surgeon separates the mesentery from the duodenum and the head of the pancreas using a 30-degree 10 mm scope and two 5 mm instruments, without additional instruments for retraction.

The surgeon completes the division and mobilization of the proximal transverse colon, using a gas piece placed below to assist in the process. The lateral attachment of the right side colon is divided, marking the final separation of the colon.

The surgeon maintains the gyrotous fascia layer over the dissected area. The small bowel mesentery is prepared along the line of division of the ileocolic vessel, reaching completion. Similarly, the transverse colon is prepared, with the head of the pancreas clearly visible in the infracolic area.
The surgeon removes the resected specimen and performs an iliotransverse anastomosis using a surgical stapler. The final view of the surgical site is shown, with no drain placed.
